text: What is meiosis?
choices: A two-division process that produces haploid gametes from a diploid cell
A one-step process that produces two identical diploid daughter cells
The process by which DNA is transcribed into RNA
The fusion of two gametes to form a zygote
explain: Meiosis is the reduction division used in sexual reproduction to form haploid gametes.
choice_explanations: Correct. Meiosis includes meiosis I and meiosis II and reduces chromosome number.
Incorrect. That describes mitosis, not meiosis.
Incorrect. That describes transcription.
Incorrect. That describes fertilization.

text: What is the major outcome of meiosis I?
choices: The nuclear envelope reforms around a zygote
Sister chromatids separate, producing identical diploid cells
DNA is replicated for a second time
Homologous chromosomes separate, reducing chromosome number
explain: Meiosis I is the reductional division because homologous chromosomes separate.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. That is not the main outcome of meiosis I.
Incorrect. Sister chromatids separate in meiosis II, not meiosis I.
Incorrect. DNA is replicated before meiosis, not between meiosis I and II.
Correct. Separation of homologous chromosomes reduces ploidy.

text: What happens during meiosis II?
choices: Sister chromatids separate
Fertilization restores diploidy
DNA replicates again
Homologous chromosomes pair by synapsis
explain: Meiosis II resembles mitosis because sister chromatids separate.
choice_explanations: Correct. Meiosis II separates sister chromatids.
Incorrect. Fertilization is a separate process.
Incorrect. DNA is not replicated between meiosis I and II.
Incorrect. Synapsis occurs in prophase I.

text: What are homologous chromosomes?
choices: The sex chromosomes only
A pair of chromosomes with the same genes, one from each parent
Chromosomes found only in gametes
Two identical copies of a chromosome made during replication
explain: Homologous chromosomes carry the same genes at corresponding loci but may have different alleles.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Homologous chromosomes include autosomes and can include sex chromosomes in some contexts.
Correct. Homologous chromosomes come in maternal and paternal versions.
Incorrect. Homologous chromosomes are found in diploid cells, not only gametes.
Incorrect. That describes sister chromatids.

text: What are sister chromatids?
choices: Identical copies of a chromosome joined at the centromere
Haploid cells produced by meiosis
A maternal chromosome and a paternal chromosome paired together
Two nonhomologous chromosomes aligned in metaphase I
explain: Sister chromatids are replicated copies of one chromosome that remain attached until separation.
choice_explanations: Correct. Sister chromatids form after DNA replication.
Incorrect. That describes gametes.
Incorrect. That describes homologous chromosomes.
Incorrect. Nonhomologous chromosomes do not form sister chromatids.

text: What is synapsis?
choices: The joining of gametes during fertilization
The separation of sister chromatids during meiosis II
The breakdown of spindle fibers in telophase
The pairing of homologous chromosomes during prophase I
explain: Synapsis occurs in prophase I and allows homologs to line up closely.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. That describes fertilization.
Incorrect. Sister chromatid separation occurs later.
Incorrect. Synapsis is unrelated to spindle breakdown.
Correct. Synapsis is the close pairing of homologous chromosomes.

text: What is a tetrad in meiosis?
choices: A paired set of homologous chromosomes containing four chromatids
A group of four alleles at one locus
A haploid gamete with four chromosomes
Four nonhomologous chromosomes clustered together
explain: A tetrad forms when homologous chromosomes pair during prophase I.
choice_explanations: Correct. The two homologs together contain four chromatids.
Incorrect. A tetrad refers to chromosomes, not alleles.
Incorrect. A gamete is not called a tetrad.
Incorrect. Tetrads involve homologous, not nonhomologous, chromosomes.

text: What is a chiasma?
choices: The location where sister chromatids attach at the centromere
The site where ribosomes bind mRNA
The region where spindle fibers form in G1
The visible point where non-sister chromatids exchange segments
explain: A chiasma marks where crossing over occurred between homologous chromosomes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Sister chromatids attach at centromeres, not chiasmata.
Incorrect. Ribosomes do not bind at a chiasma.
Incorrect. Chiasmata are not formed in G1.
Correct. Chiasmata indicate recombination sites.

text: What is crossing over?
choices: The copying of DNA during S phase
The exchange of DNA between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes
The movement of chromosomes toward the metaphase plate
The separation of homologous chromosomes in anaphase I
explain: Crossing over occurs during prophase I and increases genetic diversity.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. DNA replication is separate from crossing over.
Correct. Recombination exchanges segments between homologs.
Incorrect. Movement to the metaphase plate is not crossing over.
Incorrect. That describes chromosome segregation.

text: What does independent assortment describe?
choices: The random orientation of homologous chromosome pairs during metaphase I
The guaranteed inheritance of linked genes together
The exact copying of DNA during S phase
The attachment of spindle fibers to sister chromatids in mitosis only
explain: Independent assortment generates different combinations of maternal and paternal chromosomes in gametes.
choice_explanations: Correct. Homolog pairs line up independently in metaphase I.
Incorrect. Linked genes do not assort independently as often.
Incorrect. DNA replication is unrelated here.
Incorrect. That does not define independent assortment.

text: What is a gamete?
choices: A diploid body cell made by mitosis
A protein that organizes spindle fibers
A haploid reproductive cell such as a sperm or egg
A pair of homologous chromosomes
explain: Gametes carry one set of chromosomes and fuse during fertilization.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Somatic cells are diploid body cells.
Incorrect. A gamete is a cell, not a protein.
Correct. Gametes are the reproductive cells in sexual organisms.
Incorrect. Homologous chromosomes are not gametes.

text: What does haploid mean?
choices: Having only sex chromosomes
Having no DNA
Having one set of chromosomes
Having two sets of chromosomes
explain: Haploid cells contain one chromosome set, as in gametes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Haploid refers to chromosome sets, not only sex chromosomes.
Incorrect. Haploid cells still contain DNA.
Correct. Haploid cells have one set of chromosomes.
Incorrect. That describes diploid cells.

text: What does diploid mean?
choices: Having chromosomes that cannot replicate
Having four sets of chromosomes only
Having two sets of chromosomes
Having one set of chromosomes
explain: Diploid cells contain two sets of chromosomes, one from each parent.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Diploid chromosomes can replicate.
Incorrect. Four sets would be tetraploid, not diploid.
Correct. Diploid cells are 2n.
Incorrect. That describes haploid cells.

text: What is fertilization?
choices: The division of a somatic cell into two identical cells
The fusion of two haploid gametes to form a diploid zygote
The replication of DNA before meiosis
The separation of homologous chromosomes during meiosis I
explain: Fertilization restores diploidy by combining chromosome sets from two parents.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. That describes mitosis.
Correct. Fertilization fuses gametes and forms a zygote.
Incorrect. That describes DNA replication.
Incorrect. That describes meiosis I.

text: What is a zygote?
choices: The diploid cell formed when two gametes fuse
A haploid gamete before fertilization
A cell produced after meiosis II only
A homologous chromosome pair during meiosis
explain: A zygote is the first diploid cell of a new sexually produced organism.
choice_explanations: Correct. The zygote forms at fertilization.
Incorrect. A gamete is not a zygote.
Incorrect. A zygote forms by fertilization, not after meiosis II only.
Incorrect. Homologous chromosomes are not zygotes.

text: What does Mendel's law of segregation state?
choices: The two alleles for a gene separate during gamete formation
Sister chromatids never separate in meiosis
Dominant alleles are always more common in populations
All genes are inherited as linked blocks
explain: Segregation means each gamete receives only one allele of each gene.
choice_explanations: Correct. Alleles separate when gametes form.
Incorrect. Sister chromatids do separate in meiosis II.
Incorrect. Dominance does not determine population frequency.
Incorrect. Some genes are linked, but not all.

text: What does Mendel's law of independent assortment state?
choices: Dominant alleles always assort away from recessive alleles
Genes on the same chromosome always assort independently
Every allele pair stays together during meiosis
Alleles of different genes assort independently when the genes are on different chromosomes
explain: Independent assortment applies most directly to genes on different chromosomes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Dominance does not determine assortment direction.
Incorrect. Linked genes on the same chromosome do not usually assort independently.
Incorrect. Alleles do not all stay together.
Correct. Genes on different chromosomes assort independently in meiosis.

text: What is a genotype?
choices: The observable traits of an organism only
The number of chromosomes in a gamete only
The set of alleles an organism has for one or more genes
The environment in which an organism develops
explain: Genotype refers to genetic makeup, while phenotype refers to observable expression.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. That describes phenotype.
Incorrect. Genotype is broader than chromosome number in a gamete.
Correct. Genotype is the allele combination.
Incorrect. Environment can affect phenotype but is not genotype.

text: What is a phenotype?
choices: The chromosome number only
The DNA sequence of one gamete only
The observable expression of traits
The set of alleles inherited for a gene
explain: Phenotype is what is observed and can be influenced by genotype and environment.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Phenotype is broader than chromosome number.
Incorrect. A phenotype is not just a gamete DNA sequence.
Correct. Phenotype refers to observable traits.
Incorrect. That describes genotype.

text: What does homozygous mean?
choices: Having only one chromosome in the cell
Having two different alleles for a gene
Having no allele for a gene
Having two of the same allele for a gene
explain: A homozygous genotype has matching alleles at a locus.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. That refers to ploidy, not homozygosity.
Incorrect. That describes heterozygous.
Incorrect. Diploid organisms generally carry alleles at the locus.
Correct. Homozygous means same allele pair.

text: What does heterozygous mean?
choices: Having no inherited alleles
Having replicated chromosomes only
Having two different alleles for a gene
Having two identical alleles for a gene
explain: A heterozygous genotype contains two different alleles at the same locus.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. The organism still has alleles.
Incorrect. Chromosome replication does not define heterozygosity.
Correct. Heterozygous means the two alleles differ.
Incorrect. That describes homozygous.

text: What is a dominant allele?
choices: An allele that prevents meiosis
An allele found only on the Y chromosome
An allele that is always more common in a population
An allele whose trait is expressed in a heterozygote
explain: A dominant allele can determine phenotype when only one copy is present.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Dominant alleles do not prevent meiosis.
Incorrect. Dominance is not restricted to Y-linked genes.
Incorrect. Dominance does not guarantee high frequency.
Correct. Dominant alleles affect phenotype in heterozygotes.

text: What is a recessive allele?
choices: An allele whose trait is masked in a heterozygote by a dominant allele
An allele that always increases fitness
An allele that is never inherited
An allele found only in diploid cells
explain: A recessive phenotype usually appears only when two recessive alleles are present.
choice_explanations: Correct. Recessive alleles are not expressed in typical heterozygotes.
Incorrect. Recessive alleles do not always affect fitness this way.
Incorrect. Recessive alleles can still be inherited.
Incorrect. Recessive alleles can exist in gametes too.

text: What is a monohybrid cross?
choices: A genetic cross that tracks inheritance of one gene
A cell division that produces gametes
A chromosome mutation caused by radiation
A cross involving four genes at once
explain: A monohybrid cross focuses on one gene or trait.
choice_explanations: Correct. Monohybrid means one trait is followed.
Incorrect. That describes meiosis.
Incorrect. A monohybrid cross is a genetics cross, not a mutation event.
Incorrect. That would involve multiple genes.

text: What is a dihybrid cross?
choices: A cross involving only one allele
A test for environmental effects only
The pairing of homologous chromosomes in meiosis
A genetic cross that tracks inheritance of two genes
explain: A dihybrid cross examines the inheritance of two different genes or traits.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. It involves two genes, not one allele.
Incorrect. A dihybrid cross is not limited to environment.
Incorrect. Pairing of homologs is synapsis, not a dihybrid cross.
Correct. Dihybrid means two traits are followed.

text: What is a test cross used for?
choices: To make homologous chromosomes exchange alleles
To determine an unknown genotype by crossing with a homozygous recessive individual
To double the chromosome number of a zygote
To prove a trait is always sex-linked
explain: A test cross reveals whether a dominant-phenotype individual is homozygous or heterozygous.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Crossing over occurs in meiosis, not in a test cross.
Correct. Test crosses help infer genotype.
Incorrect. A test cross does not double chromosome number.
Incorrect. A test cross does not automatically prove sex linkage.

text: What is a Punnett square?
choices: A diagram used to predict possible offspring genotypes and phenotypes
A table for measuring ATP production
A karyotype of a cell in metaphase
A chart showing the stages of meiosis only
explain: Punnett squares organize gamete combinations to predict inheritance outcomes.
choice_explanations: Correct. Punnett squares are a genetics prediction tool.
Incorrect. ATP production is unrelated.
Incorrect. A karyotype is a chromosome display, not a Punnett square.
Incorrect. They are not limited to meiosis stages.

text: What does the product rule in probability calculate?
choices: The probability that two independent events both occur
The number of recombinant offspring only
The expected chromosome number after meiosis
The probability that either of two mutually exclusive events occurs
explain: The product rule multiplies probabilities of independent events occurring together.
choice_explanations: Correct. Multiply probabilities for independent events happening together.
Incorrect. The product rule is broader than recombinant offspring.
Incorrect. Chromosome number is not calculated with the product rule.
Incorrect. That describes the addition rule.

text: What does the addition rule in probability calculate?
choices: The chromosome number of a haploid cell
The probability that independent events all occur together
The probability that one of several mutually exclusive events occurs
The crossing-over frequency between linked genes
explain: The addition rule adds the probabilities of mutually exclusive outcomes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Haploid number is not calculated by the addition rule.
Incorrect. That describes the product rule.
Correct. Add probabilities when events cannot happen at the same time.
Incorrect. Crossing-over frequency is not the addition rule.

text: What is an autosomal trait?
choices: A trait determined by a gene on a non-sex chromosome
A trait determined only by the Y chromosome
A trait determined only by mitochondrial DNA
A trait that occurs only in males
explain: Autosomal genes are found on autosomes, not X or Y chromosomes.
choice_explanations: Correct. Autosomal means the gene is on a non-sex chromosome.
Incorrect. Y-linked traits are not autosomal.
Incorrect. Mitochondrial inheritance is different.
Incorrect. Autosomal traits can occur in any sex.

text: What is a sex-linked trait?
choices: A trait controlled by a gene located on a sex chromosome
A trait inherited only from the father
A trait caused only by environment
A trait found only in diploid cells
explain: Sex-linked traits are determined by genes on the X or Y chromosome.
choice_explanations: Correct. Sex-linked genes are on sex chromosomes.
Incorrect. Sex-linked traits are not always inherited from the father.
Incorrect. Environmental traits are different.
Incorrect. Sex-linked genes can be present in gametes and diploid cells.

text: What is a pedigree?
choices: A family chart used to trace inheritance of a trait across generations
A probability equation for independent events
A chart that shows meiosis stages only
A diagram of chromosomes arranged by size
explain: Pedigrees help infer inheritance patterns such as autosomal or sex-linked transmission.
choice_explanations: Correct. Pedigrees track traits through families.
Incorrect. A pedigree is not an equation.
Incorrect. A pedigree is not a meiosis-stage diagram.
Incorrect. That describes a karyotype.

text: What is a carrier in genetics?
choices: An individual missing one chromosome from every cell
A cell that transfers ATP to offspring
An individual who has a recessive allele but does not show the recessive phenotype
An individual with two dominant alleles only
explain: A carrier is usually heterozygous for a recessive allele and can pass it to offspring.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Chromosome loss is not the definition of a carrier.
Incorrect. ATP transfer is unrelated.
Correct. Carriers can transmit recessive alleles without expressing them.
Incorrect. A carrier is typically heterozygous, not homozygous dominant.

text: What are linked genes?
choices: Genes that exist only in mitochondria
Genes located on different chromosomes that always assort independently
Genes found only in homozygous individuals
Genes located close together on the same chromosome
explain: Linked genes tend to be inherited together unless crossing over separates them.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Linked genes are not limited to mitochondria.
Incorrect. Genes on different chromosomes are not linked.
Incorrect. Linkage is unrelated to homozygosity.
Correct. Physical proximity on a chromosome creates linkage.

text: What does recombination frequency measure?
choices: How frequently dominant alleles appear in a population
How often crossing over separates linked genes
How often homologous chromosomes fail to replicate
How many gametes an organism can make in one day
explain: Recombination frequency estimates how often recombinant gametes are produced.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Dominant allele frequency is not recombination frequency.
Correct. It is used to study linkage and mapping.
Incorrect. It is not about replication failure.
Incorrect. Gamete production rate is different.

text: What is one map unit, or centimorgan?
choices: A single nucleotide mutation
One homologous pair in metaphase I
One complete chromosome replication
A recombination frequency of 1 percent between two genes
explain: Map units estimate gene distance based on how often recombination occurs.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. It does not describe a single mutation.
Incorrect. It is not a chromosome pair.
Incorrect. A map unit is not a replication event.
Correct. One centimorgan equals 1% recombination.

text: What is gene mapping?
choices: Using pedigrees to count only dominant individuals
Measuring how many chromosomes are in one gamete
Using recombination data to estimate distances between genes on a chromosome
Copying genes from DNA into RNA
explain: Gene mapping uses recombination frequency to estimate relative gene positions.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Gene mapping is not just counting dominant individuals.
Incorrect. Chromosome counting alone is not gene mapping.
Correct. Recombination data can reveal relative distance between linked genes.
Incorrect. That describes transcription.

text: What is incomplete dominance?
choices: A pattern in which linked genes assort independently
A pattern in which the heterozygote shows an intermediate phenotype
A pattern in which both alleles are fully and separately expressed
A pattern in which only Y-linked traits are expressed
explain: In incomplete dominance, neither allele completely masks the other in a heterozygote.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Linkage is unrelated.
Correct. The heterozygote looks intermediate.
Incorrect. That describes codominance.
Incorrect. Incomplete dominance is not a Y-linked pattern.

text: What is codominance?
choices: A pattern in which both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote
A pattern in which the heterozygote is intermediate
A pattern found only in sex chromosomes
A pattern in which one allele is never inherited
explain: In codominance, both alleles contribute visibly to the phenotype.
choice_explanations: Correct. The heterozygote expresses both allele products.
Incorrect. That describes incomplete dominance.
Incorrect. Codominance is not limited to sex chromosomes.
Incorrect. Both alleles can be inherited.

text: What does the term multiple alleles mean?
choices: Only recessive alleles are present in the species
The gene is found on multiple chromosomes in one gamete
One individual can carry five alleles for one gene
A gene has more than two possible alleles in the population
explain: A population can have many alleles for a gene, even though each diploid individual carries only two.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Multiple alleles does not mean only recessive alleles exist.
Incorrect. Genes are not normally located on multiple chromosomes in one gamete.
Incorrect. A diploid individual still carries only two alleles per gene.
Correct. Multiple alleles refers to allele diversity in the population.

text: What is maternal inheritance?
choices: Inheritance of a trait through organelles passed primarily from the egg
Inheritance of a trait only from the father
Inheritance of a trait only when both parents are carriers
Inheritance of a trait through independent assortment only
explain: Mitochondrial and, in plants, chloroplast genes are typically inherited from the mother.
choice_explanations: Correct. Cytoplasmic organelles usually come from the egg.
Incorrect. Maternal inheritance is not paternal inheritance.
Incorrect. Carrier status does not define maternal inheritance.
Incorrect. Independent assortment is different.

text: Why are many mitochondrial traits maternally inherited in animals?
choices: Because mitochondria are usually contributed by the egg rather than the sperm
Because meiosis II occurs only in females
Because sperm cells have more chromosomes than eggs
Because mitochondrial genes are all dominant
explain: Animal embryos typically receive mitochondria from the egg cytoplasm.
choice_explanations: Correct. Most mitochondria come from the egg.
Incorrect. Meiosis II occurs in both sexes in a general sense of gamete formation.
Incorrect. Sperm and eggs each carry one chromosome set.
Incorrect. Dominance does not explain the inheritance pattern.

text: In plants, why are chloroplast traits often maternally inherited?
choices: Because chloroplast genes follow strict Mendelian independent assortment
Because chloroplasts are commonly transmitted in the ovule rather than pollen
Because pollen contains no DNA of any kind
Because chloroplasts are produced only after fertilization
explain: In many plants, chloroplasts are inherited through the ovule, producing maternal inheritance patterns.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Organellar inheritance does not follow simple Mendelian inheritance.
Correct. Chloroplasts often come from the ovule parent.
Incorrect. Pollen does carry DNA.
Incorrect. Chloroplasts do not first appear only after fertilization.

text: What is nondisjunction?
choices: The normal pairing of homologous chromosomes in prophase I
The failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis or mitosis
The fusion of gametes during fertilization
The exchange of alleles between non-sister chromatids
explain: Nondisjunction leads to gametes or cells with abnormal chromosome numbers.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. That describes synapsis.
Correct. Improper chromosome separation is nondisjunction.
Incorrect. That describes fertilization.
Incorrect. That describes crossing over.

text: What is aneuploidy?
choices: An abnormal number of one or more chromosomes
A normal diploid condition
A phenotype caused only by environment
A mutation in a single base pair only
explain: Aneuploidy results from chromosome-number errors such as nondisjunction.
choice_explanations: Correct. Aneuploid cells have missing or extra chromosomes.
Incorrect. Diploidy is a normal chromosome condition.
Incorrect. Environment alone does not define aneuploidy.
Incorrect. That describes a gene mutation, not chromosome-number change.

text: What are autosomes?
choices: All mitochondrial chromosomes
Only the Y chromosome
Only the X chromosomes in females
Chromosomes that are not sex chromosomes
explain: Autosomes include all chromosome pairs except the sex chromosomes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Mitochondrial DNA is separate from nuclear autosomes.
Incorrect. The Y chromosome is a sex chromosome.
Incorrect. X chromosomes are sex chromosomes.
Correct. Autosomes are non-sex chromosomes.

text: What are sex chromosomes?
choices: All chromosomes inherited from the mother
Only chromosomes found in gametes
The chromosomes responsible for ATP synthesis only
Chromosomes that help determine biological sex, such as X and Y
explain: Sex chromosomes include X and Y in many animals and often carry sex-linked genes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. They can come from either parent.
Incorrect. Sex chromosomes are also present in somatic cells.
Incorrect. ATP synthesis is not their sole function.
Correct. Sex chromosomes are involved in sex determination.

text: What is an X-linked trait?
choices: A trait found only in females
A trait inherited only from fathers to sons
A trait that is always dominant
A trait determined by a gene located on the X chromosome
explain: An X-linked trait is caused by a gene on the X chromosome and can show sex-specific inheritance patterns.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. X-linked traits can appear in males and females.
Incorrect. Fathers do not pass their X chromosome to sons.
Incorrect. X-linked traits can be dominant or recessive.
Correct. X-linked genes are on the X chromosome.

text: What is a Y-linked trait?
choices: A trait found equally in all offspring
A trait transmitted from mother to all children
A trait determined by a gene on the Y chromosome
A trait caused by mitochondrial DNA
explain: Y-linked traits are passed from father to son because only males carry the Y chromosome.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Y-linked traits are not inherited equally by all offspring.
Incorrect. Mothers do not pass Y-linked traits.
Correct. Y-linked genes are on the Y chromosome.
Incorrect. Mitochondrial inheritance is different.

text: What are recombinant offspring?
choices: Offspring with no variation from either parent
Offspring that always inherit only maternal chromosomes
Offspring with a new combination of alleles compared with the parental combinations
Offspring produced by mitosis only
explain: Recombinants arise from crossing over or independent assortment.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Recombinants show new combinations, not no variation.
Incorrect. Recombinants do not inherit only maternal chromosomes.
Correct. Recombinants differ from parental allele combinations.
Incorrect. Offspring are not produced by mitosis alone.

text: What is a parental allele combination?
choices: An allele combination found only in males
An allele combination already present in one of the parents
A phenotype caused only by nondisjunction
A chromosome set created only by mutation
explain: Parental combinations match the original arrangement of alleles found in the parents.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Parental combinations are not sex-specific.
Correct. Parental combinations match the parent arrangement.
Incorrect. Nondisjunction is unrelated.
Incorrect. Mutation is not required.

text: What is a phenotypic ratio?
choices: The exact order of genes on a chromosome
The ratio of chromosomes to alleles in one gamete
The ratio of dominant alleles to recessive alleles in DNA only
The relative numbers of offspring showing different observable traits
explain: A phenotypic ratio compares offspring by observed trait categories.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Gene order is addressed by mapping, not phenotypic ratio.
Incorrect. Gamete chromosome-to-allele ratio is not a phenotypic ratio.
Incorrect. A phenotypic ratio is not simply allele counts in DNA.
Correct. Phenotypic ratios are based on observable traits.

text: What is a genotypic ratio?
choices: The chromosome number after mitosis
The ratio of sperm to eggs in a population
The relative numbers of offspring with different allele combinations
The visible trait ratio only
explain: A genotypic ratio describes the expected or observed frequencies of different genotypes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Chromosome number after mitosis is not the definition.
Incorrect. Sperm and egg counts are unrelated.
Correct. Genotypic ratios focus on allele combinations.
Incorrect. That describes a phenotypic ratio.

text: What is a chi-square test used for in genetics?
choices: To determine chromosome size in a karyotype
To calculate DNA base-pair sequence exactly
To measure ATP production rate
To compare observed results with expected results
explain: A chi-square test helps evaluate whether deviations from expectation are due to chance.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Karyotypes are not measured with chi-square.
Incorrect. Chi-square does not determine exact DNA sequence.
Incorrect. ATP production is unrelated.
Correct. Chi-square tests compare observed and expected outcomes.

text: In a genetics chi-square test, what is the null hypothesis usually stating?
choices: Every dominant allele is more common than recessive alleles
The experiment must have been done incorrectly
Any difference between observed and expected results is due to chance
Linked genes always assort independently
explain: The null hypothesis assumes deviations from expectation are caused by random sampling error.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Dominance does not determine frequency.
Incorrect. Experimental error is not automatically the null hypothesis.
Correct. That is the usual null hypothesis in chi-square analysis.
Incorrect. Linked genes do not always assort independently.

text: What is phenotypic plasticity?
choices: The inheritance of traits only from the mother
The permanent change of DNA sequence by mutation
The exchange of DNA between homologous chromosomes
The ability of the same genotype to produce different phenotypes in different environments
explain: Phenotypic plasticity shows that environment can influence the expression of genotype.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Maternal inheritance is unrelated.
Incorrect. Mutation changes genotype, not plasticity.
Incorrect. That describes crossing over.
Correct. One genotype can produce different phenotypes under different conditions.

text: What does an environmental effect on phenotype mean?
choices: External conditions can influence how a genotype is expressed
Environment changes chromosome number in every case
Only genotype matters for phenotype
Phenotypes are never affected by nutrition, temperature, or light
explain: Phenotype often reflects interactions between genotype and environment.
choice_explanations: Correct. Environmental conditions can alter the phenotype produced.
Incorrect. Environment does not always change chromosome number.
Incorrect. Environment can influence phenotype too.
Incorrect. Nutrition, temperature, and light can affect phenotype.

text: What is genetic variation?
choices: Differences in DNA or allele combinations among individuals
The complete absence of mutation and meiosis
The fact that all offspring are genetically identical
The number of ATP molecules in a cell
explain: Genetic variation is the raw material for heredity and evolution.
choice_explanations: Correct. Variation includes different alleles and combinations of alleles.
Incorrect. Meiosis and mutation contribute to variation.
Incorrect. Identical offspring would reduce variation.
Incorrect. ATP count is unrelated.

text: How does random fertilization increase genetic variation?
choices: It guarantees all offspring are genetically identical
Any sperm can fuse with any egg, creating many allele combinations
It makes linked genes assort independently
It prevents crossing over in meiosis
explain: Random union of genetically different gametes produces many possible zygote combinations.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. It increases, not eliminates, variation.
Correct. Random fertilization increases the number of possible genotypes.
Incorrect. Linked genes remain linked regardless of fertilization.
Incorrect. Crossing over occurs before fertilization.

text: During which stage of meiosis does crossing over occur?
choices: Anaphase II
Telophase I
Prophase I
Metaphase II
explain: Crossing over occurs during prophase I after homologous chromosomes pair.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Anaphase II is when sister chromatids separate.
Incorrect. Telophase I occurs after homologs separate.
Correct. Recombination occurs in prophase I.
Incorrect. Metaphase II is later and involves chromosome alignment.

text: During which stage does independent assortment result from chromosome orientation?
choices: Anaphase II
Metaphase I
Telophase II
Prophase II
explain: Independent assortment results from the random orientation of homologous pairs at metaphase I.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Anaphase II is later and involves chromatid separation.
Correct. Metaphase I orientation drives independent assortment.
Incorrect. Telophase II is the end of meiosis II.
Incorrect. Prophase II is not where homolog pairs orient.

text: Why is meiosis I called a reduction division?
choices: It reduces DNA replication errors to zero
It reduces the chromosome number from diploid to haploid
It reduces ATP synthesis in gametes
It reduces chromosome number from haploid to diploid
explain: Homologous chromosome separation in meiosis I halves the chromosome number.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Meiosis I does not reduce errors to zero.
Correct. Meiosis I reduces ploidy.
Incorrect. ATP synthesis is not why it is called reduction division.
Incorrect. Haploid to diploid happens at fertilization.

text: Why is meiosis II sometimes called an equational division?
choices: Because sister chromatids separate without further reducing ploidy
Because homologous chromosomes pair and recombine
Because the cell becomes diploid again
Because fertilization occurs at the same time
explain: Meiosis II separates sister chromatids, similar to mitosis, while chromosome number stays haploid.
choice_explanations: Correct. Chromatids separate but the cell remains haploid.
Incorrect. Pairing and recombination occur in prophase I.
Incorrect. Diploidy is restored by fertilization, not meiosis II.
Incorrect. Fertilization is separate.

text: What is a locus?
choices: The number of alleles in a population
The process of chromosome separation in anaphase
The physical location of a gene on a chromosome
A type of gamete produced only in plants
explain: A locus is the position where a gene is found on a chromosome.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Population allele number is not a locus.
Incorrect. Chromosome separation is not a locus.
Correct. Locus means gene position.
Incorrect. A locus is not a gamete type.

text: What is an allele?
choices: A replicated chromatid
An alternative version of a gene
A type of spindle fiber
A complete set of chromosomes
explain: Alleles are different forms of the same gene at the same locus.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Chromatids are DNA copies, not alleles.
Correct. Alleles are alternate gene versions.
Incorrect. Spindle fibers are structural proteins.
Incorrect. A chromosome set is not an allele.

text: What is the key behavior of homologous chromosomes in meiosis I?
choices: They pair and then separate into different cells
They become sister chromatids
They remain fused permanently
They skip metaphase I completely
explain: Homologous chromosomes pair in prophase I and separate during anaphase I.
choice_explanations: Correct. Pairing and separation of homologs define meiosis I.
Incorrect. Sister chromatids are replicated copies of one chromosome, not a homolog pair.
Incorrect. Homologs do not remain fused permanently.
Incorrect. Homologs do pass through metaphase I.

text: Why are linked genes often inherited together?
choices: Because dominant genes attract recessive genes
Because they are inherited through mitochondria only
Because they are on the same chromosome and may not be separated by crossing over
Because independent assortment forces them apart every time
explain: Physical proximity on the same chromosome makes linked genes more likely to travel together.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Dominant and recessive genes do not attract each other.
Incorrect. Linkage is not limited to mitochondrial genes.
Correct. Crossing over may not separate closely linked genes.
Incorrect. Independent assortment does not usually apply to closely linked genes the same way.

text: What is true about genes that are very close together on a chromosome?
choices: They always assort independently
They can never be inherited together
They always produce codominance
They usually have a lower recombination frequency than genes farther apart
explain: The closer two genes are, the less likely crossing over will occur between them.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Close genes do not usually assort independently.
Incorrect. They are often inherited together.
Incorrect. Gene distance does not determine codominance.
Correct. Shorter distances reduce recombination frequency.

text: What is generally true about genes that are farther apart on the same chromosome?
choices: They have a higher chance of being separated by crossing over
They can no longer mutate
They are always inherited as a single unit
They become located on different chromosomes
explain: A greater distance gives more opportunity for recombination between genes.
choice_explanations: Correct. Greater distance usually increases recombination frequency.
Incorrect. Distance does not stop mutation.
Incorrect. Farther genes are less likely to stay together than close genes.
Incorrect. They remain on the same chromosome unless a chromosomal change occurs.

text: When is a recessive phenotype most often expressed?
choices: Whenever a dominant allele is present
Only in males for every gene
When an individual has two recessive alleles
Only when chromosomes fail to separate
explain: A recessive phenotype usually appears in homozygous recessive individuals.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. A dominant allele usually masks the recessive phenotype.
Incorrect. Recessive genes are not all sex-linked.
Correct. Two recessive alleles are typically needed.
Incorrect. Nondisjunction is unrelated.

text: When can a dominant phenotype be expressed?
choices: When at least one dominant allele is present
Only after crossing over
Only when two recessive alleles are present
Only in haploid cells
explain: A dominant phenotype can appear in either a homozygous dominant or heterozygous individual.
choice_explanations: Correct. One dominant allele is often enough.
Incorrect. Crossing over is unrelated to dominance expression.
Incorrect. Two recessive alleles produce recessive phenotype in simple dominance.
Incorrect. Dominant phenotypes can appear in diploid organisms too.

text: Which term refers to what can be seen or measured in an organism?
choices: Genotype
Centromere
Centimorgan
Phenotype
explain: Phenotype is the observable expression of an organism's traits.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Genotype is the allele makeup.
Incorrect. A centromere is a chromosome region.
Incorrect. A centimorgan is a map unit.
Correct. Phenotype is what is observed.

text: Which term refers to the inherited allele combination of an organism?
choices: Phenotype
Pedigree
Genotype
Synapsis
explain: Genotype refers to the inherited allele combination present in the organism.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Phenotype is the observable trait.
Incorrect. A pedigree is a family chart.
Correct. Genotype is the genetic makeup.
Incorrect. Synapsis is chromosome pairing in meiosis.

text: What is the main biological purpose of meiosis?
choices: To translate mRNA into protein
To produce haploid cells for sexual reproduction
To make identical somatic cells for growth
To combine gametes into a zygote
explain: Meiosis produces haploid gametes so chromosome number can be restored at fertilization.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Protein synthesis is translation.
Correct. Meiosis forms gametes.
Incorrect. Growth by identical somatic cells is mitosis.
Incorrect. Gamete fusion is fertilization.

text: Which process directly increases genetic variation in gametes?
choices: Mitosis
Meiosis
Translation
Binary fission
explain: Meiosis increases variation through crossing over and independent assortment.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Mitosis usually produces genetically similar cells.
Correct. Meiosis generates variation in gametes.
Incorrect. Translation does not produce gametes.
Incorrect. Binary fission is prokaryotic division.

text: How is diploid chromosome number restored after meiosis?
choices: By crossing over in prophase I
By independent assortment in metaphase I
By fertilization of two haploid gametes
By cytokinesis after meiosis II only
explain: Two haploid gametes fuse during fertilization to restore diploidy.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Crossing over increases variation but does not restore diploidy.
Incorrect. Independent assortment does not restore diploidy.
Correct. Fertilization returns the chromosome number to diploid.
Incorrect. Cytokinesis after meiosis II leaves cells haploid.

text: Which process creates new allele combinations within chromosomes?
choices: DNA polymerase proofreading
Binary fission
Telophase
Crossing over
explain: Crossing over shuffles alleles between homologous chromosomes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Proofreading preserves accuracy rather than creating allele combinations.
Incorrect. Binary fission is unrelated to meiotic recombination.
Incorrect. Telophase does not create new allele combinations.
Correct. Recombination creates new allele combinations.

text: Which process creates different combinations of whole chromosomes in gametes?
choices: Independent assortment
Translation
Mitosis
DNA replication
explain: Independent assortment mixes maternal and paternal chromosomes into gametes.
choice_explanations: Correct. Whole chromosomes assort randomly during meiosis I.
Incorrect. Translation produces proteins.
Incorrect. Mitosis usually preserves chromosome combinations.
Incorrect. DNA replication copies chromosomes.

text: How many haploid cells are usually produced from one diploid cell after meiosis?
choices: Eight
One
Four
Two
explain: One diploid starting cell typically gives rise to four haploid cells after meiosis.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Meiosis does not usually produce eight cells.
Incorrect. Meiosis usually produces more than one cell.
Correct. The standard outcome is four haploid products.
Incorrect. Two cells are produced after meiosis I only.

text: When does DNA replication occur relative to meiosis?
choices: Between meiosis I and meiosis II
After fertilization only
During anaphase I
Before meiosis begins, during interphase
explain: DNA is replicated once before meiosis, not between the two meiotic divisions.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. No DNA replication occurs between meiosis I and II.
Incorrect. Replication also occurs in cells other than zygotes.
Incorrect. Anaphase I is a separation stage, not replication.
Correct. Replication occurs in S phase before meiosis.

text: Between which chromatids does crossing over usually occur?
choices: Non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes
Sister chromatids of the same chromosome only
Two chromatids from different species only
Chromatids from unrelated cells
explain: Recombination normally occurs between non-sister chromatids in a homologous pair.
choice_explanations: Correct. Non-sister chromatids exchange corresponding segments.
Incorrect. Sister chromatids are usually identical and are not the usual crossing-over partners.
Incorrect. Species differences do not define crossing-over partners.
Incorrect. Crossing over occurs within one meiotic cell.

text: Which inheritance pattern often appears more frequently in males when recessive?
choices: Mitochondrial inheritance
Codominance
X-linked recessive inheritance
Incomplete dominance
explain: Males have only one X chromosome, so recessive X-linked alleles are often expressed more often in males.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Mitochondrial inheritance depends on maternal transmission, not male frequency specifically.
Incorrect. Codominance refers to allele expression, not sex-specific frequency.
Correct. Males need only one recessive X-linked allele to express the trait.
Incorrect. Incomplete dominance is not a sex-linked pattern.

text: Which inheritance pattern is suggested when an affected mother can pass a trait to all offspring, but an affected father passes it to none?
choices: Mitochondrial inheritance
Autosomal recessive inheritance
Y-linked inheritance
X-linked dominant inheritance
explain: Mitochondria usually come from the egg, so maternal transmission is the key clue.
choice_explanations: Correct. Maternal inheritance fits this pattern.
Incorrect. Autosomal recessive inheritance does not show this strict maternal pattern.
Incorrect. Affected fathers pass Y-linked traits only to sons, not none.
Incorrect. X-linked dominant inheritance does not produce the same all-from-mother and none-from-father pattern.

text: Why might two individuals with the same genotype show different phenotypes?
choices: They may have developed under different environmental conditions
Genotype never influences phenotype
One individual must have no alleles
The two individuals must belong to different species
explain: Environmental conditions can change gene expression and therefore phenotype.
choice_explanations: Correct. Environment can alter how the genotype is expressed.
Incorrect. Genotype strongly influences phenotype.
Incorrect. Both individuals still have alleles.
Incorrect. Different phenotypes can occur within one species too.

text: What does a large chi-square value generally suggest?
choices: Observed and expected results are nearly identical
Genes are always linked
Observed results differ substantially from expected results
The chromosome number is haploid
explain: A large chi-square indicates a bigger difference between observed and expected values.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Small differences would give a smaller chi-square value.
Incorrect. Linkage must be inferred from the context, not chi-square alone.
Correct. Large deviations increase the chi-square value.
Incorrect. Chi-square does not directly tell ploidy.

text: What does a very small chi-square value generally suggest?
choices: The null hypothesis must be rejected
The genes are definitely sex-linked
Observed results are close to expected results
All offspring are recombinant
explain: A small chi-square means the observed data fit the expected ratio fairly well.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. A very small value often supports failing to reject the null hypothesis.
Incorrect. Small chi-square does not determine sex-linkage.
Correct. Small deviations produce small chi-square values.
Incorrect. Recombinant status is unrelated to chi-square size alone.

text: Why does meiosis contribute more to variation than mitosis?
choices: It copies DNA twice before each division
It includes crossing over and independent assortment
It avoids all chromosome movement
It guarantees identical daughter cells
explain: Meiosis shuffles alleles and chromosomes, while mitosis usually preserves the original genotype.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. DNA is replicated once, not twice, before meiosis.
Correct. These two meiotic features generate variation.
Incorrect. Meiosis depends on chromosome movement.
Incorrect. Identical daughter cells describe mitosis.

text: What is one likely result of nondisjunction in meiosis?
choices: Perfectly balanced recombinant gametes only
The elimination of environmental effects on phenotype
Gametes with too many or too few chromosomes
All offspring are homozygous dominant
explain: Nondisjunction produces abnormal chromosome numbers in gametes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Nondisjunction is not normal recombination.
Incorrect. Environmental effects are unrelated.
Correct. Affected gametes may have n+1 or n−1 chromosomes.
Incorrect. Homozygous dominant offspring are not the direct result.

text: If homologous chromosomes fail to separate in meiosis I, what is the immediate problem?
choices: Crossing over increases too much
The alleles for one gene fuse permanently
The daughter cells receive unequal chromosome sets
The cell becomes diploid again
explain: Failure of homologs to separate in meiosis I causes unequal chromosome distribution.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. The direct problem is segregation, not simply more crossing over.
Incorrect. Alleles do not fuse permanently.
Correct. Homologs should move to opposite poles in meiosis I.
Incorrect. The cell is not restored to diploid by this error.

text: If sister chromatids fail to separate in meiosis II, what is the direct result?
choices: All gametes remain diploid
Homologous chromosomes undergo synapsis again
Random fertilization stops occurring
Some gametes receive an extra chromatid while others lack one
explain: Nondisjunction in meiosis II affects sister chromatid separation and alters gamete chromosome number.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Not all gametes become diploid.
Incorrect. Synapsis does not occur again in meiosis II.
Incorrect. Random fertilization is unaffected directly.
Correct. Improper chromatid separation creates abnormal gametes.

text: If two genes are 10 map units apart, what does that suggest?
choices: They are on different chromosomes
They can never be separated by crossing over
About 10 percent of gametes are recombinant for those genes
They always assort independently
explain: One map unit corresponds to about 1 percent recombination frequency.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Linked genes can still be on the same chromosome.
Incorrect. Crossing over can separate them sometimes.
Correct. A 10 cM distance suggests about 10% recombination.
Incorrect. 10 cM suggests linkage, not independent assortment.

text: Why does recombination frequency rarely exceed 50 percent?
choices: Crossing over stops all gene movement above 10 percent
A 50 percent value is the same pattern expected for genes assorting independently
Genes more than 50 map units apart cannot exist
Recessive alleles prevent recombination
explain: Genes that behave as if unlinked produce about 50 percent recombinant offspring.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. There is no 10% hard stop.
Correct. At 50% recombination, genes appear unlinked.
Incorrect. Genes can be farther apart; detection is the issue.
Incorrect. Recessive alleles do not prevent recombination.

text: Which factor is most likely an environmental variable affecting phenotype?
choices: The locus of a gene on a chromosome
The allele inherited from the father
Temperature during development
Chromosome number alone
explain: Temperature is an external condition that can influence gene expression and phenotype.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Locus is a chromosome position, not an environment.
Incorrect. Inherited allele is genetic, not environmental.
Correct. Temperature is a classic environmental variable.
Incorrect. Chromosome number is genetic, not environmental.

text: If identical twins differ in height as adults, what is the best explanation?
choices: Height is never affected by environment
They must have completely different genotypes
One twin lost all dominant alleles
Environmental influences contributed to phenotype differences
explain: Even with the same genotype, nutrition and other environmental factors can influence height.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Height is a classic example of gene-environment interaction.
Incorrect. Identical twins generally share the same genotype.
Incorrect. They do not lose all dominant alleles.
Correct. Environment can alter phenotype even in genetically similar individuals.

text: What is the centromere?
choices: The space between homologous chromosomes during meiosis
The point where a trait becomes dominant
The region where sister chromatids are attached
The site where fertilization occurs
explain: The centromere connects sister chromatids until they separate.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. The centromere is part of a chromosome, not the space between homologs.
Incorrect. Dominance is not determined by a chromosome region.
Correct. Sister chromatids remain attached at the centromere.
Incorrect. Fertilization occurs when gametes fuse, not at a centromere.

text: What does random chromosome assortment in meiosis produce?
choices: The immediate formation of a diploid zygote
Different combinations of maternal and paternal chromosomes in gametes
Genetically identical gametes every time
Only recombinant chromatids and no parental chromatids
explain: Random orientation of homologous pairs in metaphase I mixes maternal and paternal chromosomes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. A zygote forms at fertilization, not during assortment.
Correct. This contributes to genetic variation.
Incorrect. Meiosis produces variable gametes, not identical ones.
Incorrect. Both parental and recombinant chromatids can be present.

text: In a diploid organism, what is a maternal chromosome?
choices: A chromosome that can never recombine
A chromosome with only recessive alleles
A chromosome found only in eggs
A chromosome inherited from the mother
explain: Each homologous pair usually includes one maternal and one paternal chromosome.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Maternal chromosomes can recombine during meiosis.
Incorrect. Maternal chromosomes can carry dominant or recessive alleles.
Incorrect. Maternal chromosomes can be found in all diploid body cells.
Correct. The maternal chromosome came from the mother.

text: In a diploid organism, what is a paternal chromosome?
choices: A chromosome that cannot undergo independent assortment
A chromosome present only in sperm after meiosis
A chromosome with only dominant alleles
A chromosome inherited from the father
explain: Each homologous pair usually contains one chromosome inherited from the father.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Paternal chromosomes still undergo independent assortment.
Incorrect. Paternal chromosomes are also present in somatic diploid cells.
Incorrect. They can carry dominant or recessive alleles.
Correct. The paternal chromosome came from the father.

text: Why does sexual reproduction usually increase genetic variation more than asexual reproduction?
choices: It prevents crossing over and independent assortment
It combines meiosis and fertilization, creating new allele combinations
It always produces genetically identical offspring
It eliminates all recessive alleles from populations
explain: Sexual reproduction creates variation through meiosis and random fertilization.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Sexual reproduction includes crossing over and independent assortment.
Correct. These processes reshuffle alleles each generation.
Incorrect. Asexual reproduction is more associated with genetically similar offspring.
Incorrect. Sexual reproduction does not eliminate recessive alleles.

text: Compared with a diploid body cell, how many chromosome sets does a gamete have?
choices: Twice as many chromosome sets
Half as many chromosome sets
The same number of chromosome sets after fertilization
No chromosomes at all
explain: Gametes are haploid, so they carry one set instead of two.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Gametes do not have more chromosome sets than diploid cells.
Correct. Gametes have one chromosome set, while diploid cells have two.
Incorrect. Fertilization changes the zygote, not the gamete itself.
Incorrect. Gametes still carry chromosomes.

text: Why does synapsis in prophase I matter for heredity?
choices: It allows homologous chromosomes to pair, making crossing over possible
It directly restores diploidy before fertilization
It prevents all mutation in the gametes
It ensures sister chromatids separate in meiosis I
explain: Synapsis brings homologous chromosomes together so non-sister chromatids can exchange segments.
choice_explanations: Correct. Pairing is required for the normal crossing-over process.
Incorrect. Diploidy is restored by fertilization.
Incorrect. Synapsis does not prevent all mutation.
Incorrect. Sister chromatids separate in meiosis II, not because of synapsis.

text: A cell enters meiosis but homologous chromosomes never pair. Which outcome is most likely?
choices: The cell will automatically become diploid again after meiosis I
The gametes will be genetically identical to the parent because DNA will not replicate
Crossing over will be greatly reduced or absent, decreasing genetic diversity
Independent assortment will increase to 100 percent recombination for linked genes
explain: Without homolog pairing, recombination between non-sister chromatids cannot occur normally.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Meiosis does not restore diploidy.
Incorrect. DNA replication occurs before meiosis.
Correct. Synapsis failure disrupts recombination and can disrupt segregation as well.
Incorrect. Independent assortment does not create 100% recombination for linked genes.

text: Why does meiosis include two divisions after only one round of DNA replication?
choices: One division separates homologous chromosomes and the second separates sister chromatids
The first division removes all recessive alleles and the second removes dominant alleles
The first division restores diploidy and the second restores haploidy
Both divisions replicate DNA so each gamete has extra chromosomes
explain: Meiosis I reduces chromosome number by separating homologs, and meiosis II separates sister chromatids.
choice_explanations: Correct. The two divisions have different chromosome-separation roles.
Incorrect. Meiosis does not sort chromosomes by dominance.
Incorrect. Meiosis does not restore diploidy.
Incorrect. DNA is replicated only once before meiosis.

text: Which event is the clearest sign that a cell is in anaphase I rather than anaphase II?
choices: Sister chromatids separate and move to opposite poles
Homologous chromosomes move to opposite poles while sister chromatids stay joined
Chromosomes line up singly at the metaphase plate
DNA replication produces identical chromatids
explain: Anaphase I separates homologous chromosomes, unlike anaphase II, which separates sister chromatids.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. That describes anaphase II or mitosis.
Correct. Homolog separation defines anaphase I.
Incorrect. Single-file alignment is more characteristic of metaphase II or mitosis.
Incorrect. DNA replication occurs before meiosis.

text: Why does crossing over not change chromosome number even though it changes allele combinations?
choices: It occurs after fertilization, when chromosome number is already fixed
It doubles the number of chromosomes and then halves it again immediately
It exchanges corresponding DNA segments between homologous chromosomes without adding or removing whole chromosomes
It replaces one homologous chromosome with another
explain: Recombination changes which alleles are linked together on a chromatid, but chromosome number stays the same.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Crossing over occurs in meiosis, not after fertilization.
Incorrect. Crossing over does not double chromosome number.
Correct. Segment exchange changes combinations, not chromosome count.
Incorrect. The chromosomes exchange segments; they are not replaced.

text: Why are homologous chromosomes, rather than sister chromatids, separated first in meiosis?
choices: Separating homologs first reduces chromosome number from diploid to haploid
Separating sister chromatids first would increase crossing over
Homologs separate first to restore the zygote chromosome number
Separating homologs first guarantees all gametes are identical
explain: The reduction from 2n to n happens when homologous chromosomes separate in meiosis I.
choice_explanations: Correct. This is why meiosis I is the reduction division.
Incorrect. Sister chromatid order does not increase crossing over.
Incorrect. Zygote chromosome number is restored by fertilization.
Incorrect. Meiosis does not produce identical gametes.

text: Why does the random orientation of homologous pairs at metaphase I increase variation?
choices: It guarantees a 3:1 phenotypic ratio in all offspring
It causes every linked gene pair to recombine
Each gamete can receive different mixtures of maternal and paternal homologs
It prevents sister chromatids from separating later
explain: Different orientations create different chromosome combinations in the resulting gametes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. A 3:1 ratio is specific to certain monohybrid crosses, not metaphase I orientation alone.
Incorrect. Orientation does not guarantee recombination of linked genes.
Correct. Independent assortment reshuffles whole chromosomes.
Incorrect. Sister chromatids still separate in meiosis II.

text: A student says meiosis II is unnecessary because chromosome number is already halved after meiosis I. Which is the best response?
choices: Meiosis II is still needed to separate sister chromatids so each gamete gets one chromatid from each chromosome
Meiosis II is needed only to replicate DNA a second time
Meiosis II restores homologous chromosome pairs before fertilization
Meiosis II exists mainly to make crossing over happen
explain: After meiosis I, chromosomes still consist of two sister chromatids, so meiosis II separates them.
choice_explanations: Correct. Meiosis II completes gamete formation by separating sister chromatids.
Incorrect. DNA is not replicated between the divisions.
Incorrect. Homolog pairs are not restored until fertilization.
Incorrect. Crossing over occurs in prophase I.

text: Why is prophase I of meiosis more important for genetic diversity than prophase of mitosis?
choices: Mitosis does not involve DNA at all
Only meiosis I includes spindle fibers
Homologous chromosomes pair and can exchange DNA in meiosis I
Only meiosis I includes chromosome condensation
explain: Mitosis lacks homolog pairing and the crossing-over process that occurs in prophase I of meiosis.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Mitosis absolutely involves DNA.
Incorrect. Both processes involve spindle fibers.
Correct. Recombination is a key meiosis-specific source of variation.
Incorrect. Chromosome condensation occurs in both.

text: Which statement best explains why sister chromatids are not genetically identical after crossing over?
choices: Independent assortment changes DNA sequence within a chromatid
Crossing over replaces entire chromosomes with unrelated chromosomes
Sister chromatids mutate at identical rates only in meiosis II
Crossing over can exchange DNA between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes before sister chromatids separate
explain: After recombination, chromatids can carry a mixture of maternal and paternal segments.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Independent assortment shuffles chromosomes, not nucleotide sequence within a chromatid.
Incorrect. Entire chromosomes are not replaced.
Incorrect. Mutation rates are not the main point here.
Correct. Recombination changes the chromatid's allele combination.

text: If an organism with a dominant phenotype is crossed in a test cross with a homozygous recessive individual and the offspring show a 1:1 dominant-to-recessive phenotypic ratio, which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: The trait must show codominance
The unknown parent is homozygous dominant
The trait must be sex-linked
The unknown parent is heterozygous
explain: A 1:1 ratio in a test cross indicates the dominant-phenotype parent is heterozygous.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Codominance is not required to explain this pattern.
Incorrect. AA × aa would produce all dominant offspring.
Incorrect. A 1:1 ratio alone does not prove sex-linkage.
Correct. Aa × aa produces half dominant and half recessive offspring.

text: If an organism with a dominant phenotype is crossed in a test cross with a homozygous recessive individual and all offspring are dominant, what is the best conclusion about the unknown genotype?
choices: The recessive allele was not inherited
Independent assortment failed
The unknown parent is most likely homozygous dominant
The unknown parent must be heterozygous
explain: All dominant offspring in a test cross are most consistent with a homozygous dominant unknown parent.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. The recessive allele can still be inherited from the tester.
Incorrect. This pattern does not imply assortment failure.
Correct. AA × aa gives all dominant offspring.
Incorrect. A heterozygous parent would be expected to produce recessive offspring as well.

text: Why is a 3:1 phenotypic ratio expected in the offspring of a simple Aa × Aa monohybrid cross under complete dominance?
choices: Because one fourth of the offspring are expected to be heterozygous and the rest are homozygous recessive
Because one fourth of the offspring are expected to be aa, while the other three fourths have at least one dominant allele
Because all offspring are expected to have different genotypes but identical phenotypes
Because dominant alleles are more likely to segregate into gametes than recessive alleles
explain: An Aa × Aa cross produces a 1:2:1 genotype ratio; with complete dominance, AA and Aa look the same, creating a 3:1 phenotypic ratio.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Heterozygotes are expected to be half the offspring, not one fourth.
Correct. The genotype ratio becomes a 3:1 phenotype ratio under complete dominance.
Incorrect. Genotypes are not all different, and phenotypes are not all identical.
Incorrect. Alleles segregate equally in meiosis.

text: Why is the genotype ratio often more informative than the phenotype ratio when evaluating a simple dominant-recessive trait?
choices: Because only genotype ratios can be tested with probability
Because phenotype ratios are never measurable in real organisms
Because genotype ratios are unaffected by meiosis
Because different genotypes can produce the same phenotype under complete dominance
explain: AA and Aa can share the same phenotype, so phenotypes can hide important genetic differences.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Probability applies to both genotype and phenotype predictions.
Incorrect. Phenotypes are commonly measured.
Incorrect. Genotype ratios arise from meiosis and fertilization.
Correct. Phenotypes can mask genotype differences under complete dominance.

text: Why does Mendel's law of independent assortment apply most directly to genes on different chromosomes?
choices: Because genes on the same chromosome never recombine
Because genes on different chromosomes always cross over together
Because different homologous chromosome pairs align independently in meiosis I
Because dominant genes assort independently but recessive genes do not
explain: Independent assortment reflects the independent orientation of different chromosome pairs at metaphase I.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Genes on the same chromosome can recombine, but the law applies most directly to genes on different chromosomes.
Incorrect. Genes on different chromosomes do not cross over together.
Correct. Different chromosome pairs orient independently in meiosis I.
Incorrect. Dominance does not determine assortment.

text: If crossing over does not occur during prophase I, which outcome is most likely?
choices: Gametes will become diploid because fertilization has already occurred
The cell will automatically switch to mitosis and produce identical gametes
All linked genes will begin assorting independently in every chromosome
Gametes will still be haploid, but they will contain fewer new allele combinations within chromosomes
explain: Crossing over mainly increases diversity by reshuffling alleles, not by changing chromosome number.

text: Why are parental offspring classes usually more common than recombinant classes when genes are linked?

Incorrect. Recombinant offspring are not inherently sterile.
