Because organelle genes are always dominant
Because organelle genes never mutate
explain: Organelle inheritance can be uniparental, especially maternal, so the trait does not follow typical nuclear Mendelian patterns.
choice_explanations: Correct. Uniparental organelle inheritance creates non-Mendelian transmission patterns.
Incorrect. Organelle inheritance is not the same as nuclear chromosome segregation.
Incorrect. Dominance does not explain the transmission pattern.
Incorrect. Organelle genes can mutate.

text: Why might a pedigree be less useful for predicting exact phenotype when a trait is strongly environment-dependent?
choices: Because pedigrees cannot track inheritance at all
Because dominant alleles disappear in new environments
Because relatives with similar genotypes can still show different phenotypes under different conditions
Because environment changes chromosome number every generation
explain: Environmental effects can blur the direct relationship between genotype and phenotype within families.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Pedigrees can still reveal inheritance patterns.
Incorrect. New environments do not make dominant alleles disappear.
Correct. Similar genotypes may not look identical when environments differ.
Incorrect. Environment does not change chromosome number every generation.

text: Why does crossing over create diversity that independent assortment alone cannot?
choices: Because it doubles chromosome number before division
Because it makes every gamete recombinant for every gene
Because it reshuffles alleles within the same chromosome, not just whole maternal and paternal chromosomes
Because it occurs after fertilization
explain: Independent assortment shuffles whole chromosomes, while crossing over makes new allele combinations within chromosomes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Crossing over does not double chromosome number.
Incorrect. Not every gamete becomes recombinant for every gene.
Correct. Crossing over adds a second layer of variation.
Incorrect. Crossing over occurs during meiosis, not after fertilization.

text: Why does random fertilization multiply the variation already generated by meiosis?
choices: Because fertilization causes crossing over to start again
Because fertilization cancels out independent assortment
Because each genetically unique sperm can potentially combine with many genetically unique eggs
Because fertilization makes all offspring homozygous
explain: The random combination of varied gametes creates many possible zygote genotypes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Crossing over occurs during meiosis, not fertilization.
Incorrect. Fertilization does not cancel independent assortment.
Correct. Meiosis generates varied gametes, and fertilization combines them in many ways.
Incorrect. Fertilization does not make all offspring homozygous.

text: Which clue most strongly argues against a Y-linked trait?
choices: Affected fathers pass the trait to sons
The trait appears in both males and females
Only males are affected
The trait is inherited from father to son
explain: A Y-linked trait should affect only males because only males have a Y chromosome.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Passing from father to son is expected for Y-linkage.
Correct. Female expression argues strongly against Y-linkage.
Incorrect. Male-only expression is consistent with Y-linkage.
Incorrect. Father-to-son transmission is consistent with Y-linkage.

text: Why do homologous chromosomes need to separate correctly in meiosis I for heredity to work properly?
choices: Correct separation makes all offspring genetically identical
Correct separation prevents all recombination
Correct separation ensures every gamete remains diploid
Each gamete must receive one chromosome from each homologous pair
explain: Proper homolog separation is required so each gamete gets one chromosome from each pair and remains haploid.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Correct separation does not make offspring identical.
Incorrect. Correct separation does not prevent recombination.
Incorrect. Gametes should be haploid, not diploid.
Correct. Each gamete should receive one homolog from each pair.

text: Why is it incorrect to say that dominant alleles are always more common in a population than recessive alleles?
choices: Dominance describes phenotype expression, not how common an allele is in a population
Population frequency is determined only by chromosome number
Recessive alleles are always favored by natural selection
Dominant alleles cannot be inherited by homozygous recessive individuals
explain: An allele can be dominant yet rare, or recessive yet common. Dominance is about expression in heterozygotes.
choice_explanations: Correct. Dominance and allele frequency are different concepts.
Incorrect. Population frequency is not determined only by chromosome number.
Incorrect. Recessive alleles are not always favored.
Incorrect. This does not address allele frequency.

text: If a crossover occurs outside the interval between two genes, what is the effect on recombination between those two genes?
choices: It guarantees those genes become recombinant
It may have no effect on whether those two genes are recombinant relative to each other
It makes the genes assort independently forever
It doubles the map distance immediately
explain: Only crossovers between the two loci affect whether the alleles at those loci become recombinant.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Location matters; not every crossover affects the gene pair.
Correct. A crossover must occur between the loci to change their association.
Incorrect. A single crossover does not permanently change their inheritance pattern.
Incorrect. Map distance is inferred from many meioses, not one event.

text: Why can nondisjunction be described as a chromosome-number error rather than a gene-expression pattern?
choices: It happens only in organisms with codominance
It changes how many copies of a chromosome are present, not simply whether an allele is dominant or recessive
It affects only mitochondrial genes
It always creates intermediate phenotypes
explain: Nondisjunction alters chromosome count and gene dosage rather than the dominance relationship between alleles.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Nondisjunction is not limited to codominance cases.
Correct. The main problem is chromosome copy number.
Incorrect. It affects nuclear chromosome segregation too.
Incorrect. Intermediate phenotypes describe incomplete dominance, not nondisjunction.

text: Why can reaction to the environment complicate simple genotype-to-phenotype predictions?
choices: Environmental conditions rewrite all DNA sequences in every cell
The same allele combination can produce different outcomes under different conditions
Environment prevents meiosis from occurring
Genes stop segregating correctly when the environment changes
explain: Environmental variation can change how the same genotype is expressed.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Environment does not rewrite all DNA sequences in every cell.
Correct. Environment can change the phenotype produced by a genotype.
Incorrect. Environmental change does not generally prevent meiosis.
Incorrect. Segregation is a meiotic process, not canceled by environment.

text: Why does the appearance of affected sons from unaffected mothers often suggest a recessive X-linked trait?
choices: A mother can carry a recessive allele on one X chromosome without expressing the trait and pass it to sons
Unaffected mothers cannot carry dominant alleles
Sons inherit a Y chromosome from their mothers
Only mothers contribute genes for recessive traits
explain: Carrier mothers can pass a recessive X-linked allele to sons, who express it because they have only one X chromosome.
choice_explanations: Correct. This is a classic clue for X-linked recessive inheritance.
Incorrect. Dominant alleles are not the point here.
Incorrect. Sons receive the Y chromosome from their fathers.
Incorrect. Both parents contribute genes; the pattern is about X inheritance.

text: Why is recombination frequency useful for estimating gene distance but not for proving exact physical DNA length?
choices: Because map units measure mutation rate only
Because physical DNA length never affects recombination
Because recombination frequency is identical for every chromosome in every species
Because it reflects how often crossing over separates loci, which tracks relative distance rather than exact base-pair count
explain: Map units are based on recombination frequency and estimate relative, not exact physical, distance.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Map units do not measure only mutation rate.
Incorrect. Physical distance often does influence recombination likelihood.
Incorrect. Recombination rates vary.
Correct. Gene maps are relative maps built from recombination data.

text: Why does a homozygous recessive tester make a test cross especially informative?
choices: The tester causes the alleles in the unknown parent to segregate unequally
The tester always makes the offspring homozygous dominant
Any dominant phenotype in the offspring must come from the unknown parent
The tester prevents crossing over in meiosis
explain: Because the tester contributes only recessive alleles, the unknown parent's gametes are revealed clearly in offspring phenotypes.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Segregation remains equal.
Incorrect. Offspring are not all homozygous dominant.
Correct. The tester's genotype helps expose the unknown genotype.
Incorrect. Test crosses do not prevent crossing over.

text: Why does a lack of crossing over reduce the number of possible allele combinations but not eliminate variation completely?
choices: Independent assortment and random fertilization still create variation
Gametes become identical to the parent chromosome by chromosome and allele by allele
Without crossing over, all traits become maternally inherited
Lack of crossing over prevents meiosis from reducing chromosome number
explain: Crossing over is one source of variation, but independent assortment and fertilization also contribute.
choice_explanations: Correct. Variation can still arise from other meiotic and reproductive processes.
Incorrect. Independent assortment can still create chromosome-level differences.
Incorrect. Crossing over does not determine maternal inheritance.
Incorrect. Chromosome number can still be reduced without crossing over.

text: Why can linked genes make a dihybrid cross deviate from the expected 9:3:3:1 ratio?
choices: Because linkage prevents meiosis II from occurring
Because the alleles for the two genes are not being inherited independently
Because linked genes are found only in organelles
Because linked genes automatically become codominant
explain: The 9:3:3:1 ratio assumes independent assortment, which linked genes may not follow.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Linkage does not stop meiosis II.
Correct. Linkage breaks the independent-assortment assumption of the classic ratio.
Incorrect. Linked genes are commonly nuclear genes on chromosomes.
Incorrect. Linkage does not create codominance.

text: Why can a statistically nonsignificant difference still show that observed and expected values are not exactly identical?
choices: Because the deviation may be small enough to be explained by chance even though it is not zero
Because a nonsignificant result proves the biological model is true in all conditions
Because nonsignificant means the values were exactly the same
Because statistics ignore small differences completely
explain: Failing to reject the null does not mean the values are identical; it means the deviation is not large enough to rule out chance.
choice_explanations: Correct. Small differences can still exist without being statistically strong.
Incorrect. A nonsignificant result is not absolute proof.
Incorrect. Nonsignificant does not mean exactly identical.
Incorrect. Statistics do not ignore small differences; they evaluate them.

text: Why can a chromosome-number change influence many traits at once more than a single-gene mutation usually would?
choices: Because single-gene mutations always have no effect
Because chromosome-number changes affect only one allele in one gamete
Because chromosome-number changes alter the dosage of many genes simultaneously
Because chromosome-number changes occur only after phenotype is already fixed
explain: Aneuploidy changes the number of copies of many genes, not just one locus.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Single-gene mutations can certainly have effects.
Incorrect. Chromosome-number changes affect many genes on the chromosome.
Correct. Gene dosage changes can affect many traits.
Incorrect. Chromosome changes can influence development and phenotype.

text: Why are males often used to reveal recessive X-linked alleles more directly than females?
choices: Because males have only one X chromosome, so any recessive allele on it is expressed
Because males cannot inherit dominant alleles on the X chromosome
Because females do not receive X chromosomes from their parents
Because males pass their X chromosome to sons
explain: With only one X chromosome, males are hemizygous for X-linked genes.
choice_explanations: Correct. One recessive X-linked allele can be enough for expression in males.
Incorrect. Males can inherit dominant X-linked alleles as well.
Incorrect. Females do receive X chromosomes from their parents.
Incorrect. Fathers pass the Y chromosome to sons.

text: Why would two genes with zero observed recombinants in a very small sample still need more data before concluding they are inseparable?
choices: Because recombination can be measured only in females
Because rare crossovers might simply not have been captured in the small sample
Because map units cannot be estimated from offspring data
Because zero recombinants proves the genes are on different chromosomes
explain: Small samples can miss rare recombinant classes, so more offspring may be needed.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Recombination can be studied in many systems, not only females.
Correct. Rare events may not appear in a tiny sample.
Incorrect. Offspring data are how recombination is commonly estimated.
Incorrect. Zero recombinants suggests close linkage, not different chromosomes.

text: Why might height in humans be less predictable from genotype alone than a simple Mendelian trait such as pea seed shape?
choices: Because height is always Y-linked
Because height genes do not segregate during meiosis
Because height is strongly influenced by environmental conditions as well as genotype
Because human chromosomes do not undergo independent assortment
explain: Complex traits such as height are shaped by both genes and environment.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Height is not always Y-linked.
Incorrect. Human genes do segregate during meiosis.
Correct. Environmental inputs like nutrition can influence height.
Incorrect. Human chromosomes do undergo independent assortment.

text: Why is it useful to distinguish homologous chromosomes from sister chromatids on the AP Biology exam?
choices: Because many heredity questions depend on knowing which structures separate in meiosis I versus meiosis II
Because homologous chromosomes and sister chromatids always behave identically
Because homologous chromosomes exist only after fertilization
Because only sister chromatids carry genes
explain: The distinction is essential for predicting outcomes of normal meiosis and nondisjunction errors.
choice_explanations: Correct. Stage-specific separation matters for heredity problems.
Incorrect. They do not behave identically in meiosis.
Incorrect. Homologous chromosomes exist in diploid cells before fertilization too.
Incorrect. Both carry genes.

text: Why can two genes each follow simple Mendelian dominance rules but still fail to assort independently?
choices: Because independent assortment applies only to recessive alleles
Because codominance must be present for linkage to occur
Because dominance describes allele expression, whereas assortment depends on chromosome location
Because dominance requires the genes to be on different chromosomes
explain: Expression pattern and chromosome behavior are separate issues; genes can show dominance and still be linked.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Independent assortment is not limited to recessive alleles.
Incorrect. Linkage does not require codominance.
Correct. Dominance and linkage concern different aspects of heredity.
Incorrect. Dominance does not require genes to be on different chromosomes.

text: Why can a dominant trait sometimes appear to skip a generation in a small pedigree even though dominant traits usually do not?
choices: Dominant alleles always become recessive in the next generation
The pedigree may be too small, incomplete, or affected by an individual who did not inherit the allele
The law of segregation failed in every meiosis
The dominant allele must be located in mitochondria
explain: Real pedigrees can be small or incomplete, so apparent patterns may be misleading without more family information.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Dominant alleles do not convert into recessive alleles each generation.
Correct. Incomplete family data can complicate pedigree interpretation.
Incorrect. A universal segregation failure is not supported.
Incorrect. Dominance does not imply mitochondrial location.

text: Why might a biologist repeat a genetic cross with a larger sample size after obtaining a borderline chi-square result?
choices: A larger sample changes the parental genotypes
A larger sample can help distinguish random sampling variation from a real departure from the expected model
A larger sample guarantees the null hypothesis will be rejected
A larger sample prevents all environmental effects
explain: Increasing sample size can improve the power to evaluate whether the model fits the data.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Sample size does not change the parental genotypes.
Correct. More data can clarify whether the deviation is meaningful.
Incorrect. Larger samples do not guarantee rejection.
Incorrect. Larger samples do not prevent environmental effects.

text: Which conclusion is best supported by the observation? A meiotic cell is observed in which homologous chromosomes are paired as tetrads and chiasmata are visible.
choices: the cell is in prophase II
the chromosomes are undergoing DNA replication
the cell is in prophase I
fertilization has just restored diploidy
explain: Tetrads and visible chiasmata indicate homolog pairing and recombination in prophase I.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. The chromosome arrangement does not match that stage or interpretation.
Incorrect. DNA replication occurs before meiosis, not during these observed chromosome arrangements.
Correct. Tetrads and visible chiasmata indicate homolog pairing and recombination in prophase I.
Incorrect. Fertilization is a separate event that happens after gametes form.
experiment: A student prepared microscope slides of meiotic cells and recorded the chromosome arrangements seen in different cells.

text: Based on the observation, what stage is most likely represented? A meiotic cell is observed in which homologous chromosomes are paired as tetrads and chiasmata are visible.
choices: fertilization has just restored diploidy
the cell is in prophase I
the cell is in prophase II
the chromosomes are undergoing DNA replication
explain: Tetrads and visible chiasmata indicate homolog pairing and recombination in prophase I.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Fertilization is a separate event that happens after gametes form.
Correct. Tetrads and visible chiasmata indicate homolog pairing and recombination in prophase I.
Incorrect. The chromosome arrangement does not match that stage or interpretation.
Incorrect. DNA replication occurs before meiosis, not during these observed chromosome arrangements.
experiment: Researchers stained meiotic chromosomes and compared the arrangement of chromosomes across many cells.

text: A cell is observed under the microscope, and the chromosomes are arranged as described. Which interpretation is best? A meiotic cell is observed in which homologous chromosomes are paired as tetrads and chiasmata are visible.

fertilization has just restored diploidy
the chromosomes are undergoing DNA replication
the cell is in prophase I
explain: Tetrads and visible chiasmata indicate homolog pairing and recombination in prophase I.
choice_explanations: Incorrect. The chromosome arrangement does not match that stage or interpretation.
Incorrect. Fertilization is a separate event that happens after gametes form.
Incorrect. DNA replication occurs before meiosis, not during these observed chromosome arrangements.
Correct. Tetrads and visible chiasmata indicate homolog pairing and recombination in prophase I.
experiment: An imaging study tracked chromosome behavior during meiosis using fluorescent chromosome markers.

the chromosomes are undergoing DNA replication

Incorrect. DNA replication occurs before meiosis, not during these observed chromosome arrangements.
Incorrect. Fertilization is a separate event that happens after gametes form.
Incorrect. The chromosome arrangement does not match that stage or interpretation.

text: A study reports that two gametes contain an extra copy of chromosome 7 and two gametes lack chromosome 7. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: crossing over failed in every chromosome of the cell
the trait must be inherited through mitochondria

the organism can no longer perform fertilization
explain: A meiosis I nondisjunction event typically produces four abnormal gametes.

experiment: Scientists isolated the four products of a single meiosis and counted a labeled chromosome in each gamete.

text: In a family study, unaffected parents produced an affected son and an affected daughter, and the trait later appeared in both sexes again from unaffected parents. Which inheritance pattern is best supported by the pedigree description?
choices: autosomal recessive inheritance is strongly supported
independent assortment is the only relevant conclusion
environmental effects alone fully explain the pattern
crossing over must have been absent in all meioses
explain: Affected offspring from unaffected parents in both sexes is a classic clue for autosomal recessive inheritance.
choice_explanations: Correct. Affected offspring from unaffected parents in both sexes is a classic clue for autosomal recessive inheritance.
Incorrect. Independent assortment is not an inheritance model that explains the family pattern by itself.
Incorrect. The repeated pattern across generations strongly suggests heredity rather than environment alone.
Incorrect. The pedigree pattern does not require a universal absence of crossing over.
